facts_disclosed: Burak left, opened shop in Beşiktaş, now training Deniz age 17

turns.content: I have a question about... well, training someone new. teaching a skill from the beginning. do you have advice for teaching someone who knows nothing?

Teaching from zero is one of the hardest things to do well! It depends a lot on what you're teaching, but there are some universal principles. What kind of skill are we talking about, and how old is the learner? That'll help me give more specific advice.

I am a barber... I have been cutting hair for over 40 years. and I have a new apprentice. Deniz. he is 17 years old... came to me last month. the boy cannot even hold the comb properly yet. we are starting from zero

Starting from absolute zero with a 17-year-old -- that's actually a great position to be in. He has no bad habits to unlearn, and at 17 he's old enough to take it seriously but young enough for the muscle memory to really set in.

For barbering specifically, I'd suggest building from the fundamentals:

1. **First weeks: tools and posture** -- how to hold the comb, how to hold scissors, how to stand properly (this saves his back for decades)
2. **Observation phase** -- have him watch you work for hours before he touches a head. Let him see the rhythm
3. **Practice on mannequin heads** before any real customers
4. **Start simple** -- basic trims and cleanups, not full cuts

What happened with your previous situation? Were you training someone before, or is this your first apprentice?

no no... I had Burak. he was with me for... 3 years, maybe a little more. a good kid, worked hard, learned well. but he opened his own place now... in Beşiktaş. he found a small shop there near the market

Ah, so Burak graduated from your training and went out on his own -- that's actually a huge compliment to you as a teacher. It means you trained him well enough that he felt ready to run his own shop. How do you feel about it?

I am proud... of course I am proud. this is how it should be. my master, Hasan usta, he trained me and I left too, eventually. that is the cycle... the apprentice learns, grows, leaves, becomes the master. this is the tradition. but you know... when Burak was here the shop had a rhythm. I could take a break, he could handle the simple cuts. now I am alone again and training from nothing

That's a real loss to the daily operation even when it's a proud moment. Going from having a capable second pair of hands to being back at square one is a big adjustment. You're essentially doing double duty -- running the shop solo AND teaching a beginner who can't contribute yet.

For the practical side, here's what I'd suggest for the first few months with Deniz:

- **Give him one task per week to master.** Week one: sweeping, cleaning tools, preparing hot towels. Week two: shampooing. Build slowly.
- **Let him practice the comb hold on his own time** -- even at home, just holding and flicking the comb builds dexterity.
- **Narrate what you're doing** as you cut -- "I'm holding the scissors at this angle because..." He learns by hearing your thinking, not just seeing the motions.

yes... narrating. Hasan usta did this with me. he would talk while he cut... 'the blade knows if your hand is lazy, Mehmet.' I still hear him. 40 years later and I still hear his voice. I want Deniz to hear mine like that one day

That's a beautiful way to think about it -- passing down not just the technique but the voice, the philosophy behind it. The fact that you still hear Hasan usta's words after 40 years tells you how powerful that kind of teaching is. Deniz is lucky to have that, even if he doesn't know it yet.

At 17, he probably doesn't fully appreciate what he's getting. But in 10, 20, 30 years, he'll be telling someone, "My master Mehmet used to say..." That's legacy.

inşallah... God willing. but first... the boy needs to learn how to hold a comb, hah. yesterday I showed him the grip for the third time and he holds it like he is eating soup with a spoon. I had to walk away and drink my tea so I would not lose my patience. 'sabır acıdır, meyvesi tatlıdır'... patience is bitter but its fruit is sweet

Ha! Eating soup with a spoon -- that's a vivid image. But honestly, holding a barber comb correctly is surprisingly hard for beginners. The thumb-and-fingers control takes weeks to feel natural. 

A trick that might help: have him practice holding the comb while watching TV at home. Not cutting, not doing anything with it -- just holding it in the correct grip for an hour while he's distracted by something else. The hand learns to relax into the position when the mind isn't overthinking it.

And the tea breaks for patience? Keep those. Every master needs them.

this is a good idea... the TV trick. I will tell him. you know what is funny... Burak was the same in the beginning. I forgot this. I was frustrated with him also at first. and now he has his own shop, his own customers... so maybe Deniz will be the same. you just cannot see the finished product when you are looking at the raw material

Exactly -- and that's the hardest part of being a teacher. You can see where they need to be but you're watching them fumble with the basics. Burak is proof that your method works though. Three years with you and he was ready to run his own place. That's your track record.

Deniz will get there. The first few months are the hardest for both of you -- him because everything is new, you because you have to slow down to his speed. But you've done this before and the result speaks for itself.

yes... we begin again. that is life, isn't it. you build something, it grows, it leaves, you begin again. like planting trees. okay my friend... Deniz is sweeping the floor, I should go check he is not sweeping the hair into the corner and hiding it. you know 17-year-olds... shortcuts everywhere. teşekkürler for the advice

Ha! Go check those corners. And remember -- if he's sweeping at all on his first month, that's a start. The comb grip, the scissors, the razor -- it'll come. You're planting another tree. Good luck with Deniz, and congratulations on Burak's success. That's your work out there in Besiktas. Take care!
